schools.summary: Genesis/Bĕrēshîth echo; the timeless 'was' (ēn) reaching into eternity; anarthrous theos = qualitative full deity, not 'a god'.
The essential (not written) Word; his eternal divinity grounds the necessity of redemption through the Son.
Augustinian: the natural man cannot perceive it; the verse is grasped by faith ('letters of gold').
Chrysostom: it is God who speaks through John, so we need not trace the evangelist's background.
The Word existed before all things began to be made.

debates.question: The sense of 'beginning' — the start of creation, or beyond time into eternity?

outliers.note: Alexandrian: catalogues the many scriptural senses of 'beginning' and reads Christ himself as the Beginning/Wisdom.

themes: the Word with God / distinct person

schools.summary: egeneto ('became', the historic Incarnation) over against ēn; anarthrous 'flesh' = full human nature, the whole man; explicitly anti-Docetic (Ellicott).
The Son alone — not Father or Spirit — clothed with our flesh and shown openly; continuity with the Word of v.1.
Chrysostom: we are made sons because the Word became flesh; his glory was seen only through the Incarnation.
'Flesh' = the whole man; the beholding 'we' are the apostles; grace and truth come to us who are by nature otherwise.

sides.position: the whole man / full human nature
canvassed as possibly 'corrupt nature' or 'the body' before settling on the whole man
